data.title: Japan's Komeito Party Withdraws From Ruling Coalition
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--A major political ally of Japan's ruling Liberal Democratic Party has exited the governing coalition, ending a 26-year-long relationship in a move that could fuel political instability.

"There was a disagreement over the basic stance," regarding political funding issues that caused election losses, Komeito leader Tetsuo Saito said at a news conference Friday.

"Public trust in politics has been severely damaged," Saito said.

He said the party wouldn't support Sanae Takaichi, who was recently selected as the LDP's leader, as the nation's prime minister. Takaichi needs to gain approval in parliament to become the new premier.

The yen briefly strengthened to as high as 152.41 against the dollar before trading 0.2% higher at 152.78, FactSet data show.

  • Ronnie Harui contributed to this report

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: Ethereum Price Analysis: Bullish Momentum Fades as ETH Is Rejected at $4.5K
data.description:
Ethereum’s bullish structure has started to show early signs of exhaustion as the price retraces from the $4,400 resistance zone. Despite maintaining a strong mid-term uptrend, short-term momentum has weakened after a local bearish divergence and a recent market structure shift.

The next few days will be key to determining whether this is just a healthy correction or the start of a deeper pullback.Technical Analysis

By ShayanThe Daily Chart

On the daily timeframe, ETH remains inside a long-term ascending channel but has once again failed to break through the $4,800 resistance area. The asset is now dropping toward the channel’s lower trendline and the 100-day moving average at $4,000.

The RSI has also cooled to 49, indicating that bullish momentum has faded for now but hasn’t turned bearish yet. As long as the structure holds above $4,000, the broader trend remains intact. However, losing that level could open the door for a deeper move toward $3,400, where the next significant demand zone lies.

The 4-Hour Chart

In the 4-hour view, ETH has confirmed a Market Structure Shift (MSS) after failing to sustain higher highs near $4,800. A clear bearish divergence on RSI supported this move, showing momentum loss before the drop. The price has now entered a short-term corrective phase and could revisit the $4,200–$4,100 zone, which aligns with previous demand.

For buyers to regain control, ETH needs to reclaim $4,500 and invalidate the recent lower-high formation. Until then, short-term bias remains slightly bearish within the context of the broader bullish channel.

Sentiment Analysis Open Interest

Open interest across all exchanges remains elevated at around $28.5 billion, showing that derivatives traders are still heavily positioned despite the recent pullback. This suggests strong speculative interest, but it also means the market is vulnerable to liquidations if volatility increases.

If open interest continues to stay high while price trends lower, it could trigger a cascade of forced long liquidations before a proper bottom forms. On the flip side, a gradual cooling of open interest during this correction would signal healthy resetting of leverage, potentially setting the stage for ETH’s next leg higher.

data.title: Oil Falls as Gaza Deal Eases Geopolitical Risk Premium — Market Talk
data.description: Oil prices fall in early trade after Israel approved the U.S.-brokered deal for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. "This presents a major step toward ending the two-year war that raised the risk of supply disruptions in the oil market," analysts at ANZ Research say. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.5% to $64.88 and $60.76 a barrel, respectively, after settling 1.6% lower in the previous session. Prices are also pressured by concerns over an impending supply glut as OPEC+ continues to boost production and the latest U.S. crude stockpiles data point to weaker demand. However, further losses are limited by new U.S. sanctions on Iran, with the Treasury Department targeting more than 50 individuals, entities and vessels facilitating Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0341 ET - Oil prices fall in early trade after Israel approved the U.S.-brokered deal for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. "This presents a major step toward ending the two-year war that raised the risk of supply disruptions in the oil market," analysts at ANZ Research say. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.5% to $64.88 and $60.76 a barrel, respectively, after settling 1.6% lower in the previous session. Prices are also pressured by concerns over an impending supply glut as OPEC+ continues to boost production and the latest U.S. crude stockpiles data point to weaker demand. However, further losses are limited by new U.S. sanctions on Iran, with the Treasury Department targeting more than 50 individuals, entities and vessels facilitating Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0313 ET - Bitcoin edges higher as the prolonged U.S. government shutdown and political uncertainty elsewhere drives investors toward alternative assets. The U.S. government shutdown is well into its second week and has started to disrupt businesses, with some companies facing payment delays and considering layoffs. In France, President Emmanuel Macron is expected to name a new prime minister Friday to replace Sebastien Lecornu after he resigned less than a month into the role, although uncertainty remains elevated. Bitcoin rises 0.1% to $121,339, having reached a record high of $126,223 Monday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0248 ET - The dollar's recent recovery stalls, with the currency falling slightly after reaching a two-month high in the previous session. The dollar has strengthened this week as political concerns in Japan and France weighed on the yen and the euro. Sanae Takaichi's victory in the leadership election of Japan's ruling party prompted markets to bet on lower-for-longer interest rates. However, the yen received some reprieve after Takaichi said in an interview on Japanese TV that she has no intention of triggering an excessively weak yen. In France, President Emmanuel Macron is expected to name a new prime minister Friday, easing concerns that snap elections will be called. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 99.351 after reaching a high of 99.563 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

2347 ET - The U.S.-China tariff truce, while fragile, could continue, as a hard trade decoupling has proved undesirable for both sides, economists at Citi Research say in a note. Both countries could be strengthening their leverage ahead of a potential meeting on trade between their presidents, they say. China tightened rare-earth export controls further and added more companies to the "unreliable entity list." The U.S. is set to impose port fees on Chinese-built, owned and operated ships this month, and U.S. lawmakers have called for broader bans on chip-making equipment. Export controls, China's purchase of U.S. goods and potential investment plans, China's opening up--especially of the services sector--and fentanyl tariffs could all figure at the possible summit, they add. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

2320 ET - Tesla is best positioned among U.S. automakers to navigate China's tighter rare-earth controls, according to Wedbush analysts. "Tesla's massive presence in China on this front is a relative good sign for Musk and Co.," the analysts say in a research note. China's tightened grip will have ripple effects for the auto industry, as these raw materials are crucial for producing computer chips, brakes, seats and other critical vehicle components, they add. Automakers have long been trying to reduce their dependence on China for rare earths, but these supply chains haven't been fully integrated, the analysts note. "The bark could be worse than bite," however, as China continues to use such tactics to gain leverage in trade negotiations, they say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2249 ET - The Singapore dollar strengthens slightly against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session on a possible technical recovery after its recent weakness. Financial markets appear to have taken a pause, though the ongoing U.S. government shutdown is still unresolved, says OCBC's global markets research team. This is delaying key economic releases and contributing to near-term uncertainty, the team says. USD/SGD is 0.1% lower at 1.2982 after touching 1.3007 on Thursday, the highest intraday level since May 16, LSEG data show. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1533 ET - The volume of coffee exported from Brazil in September fell considerably from this time last year, according to data from Cecafé. The firm says that 3.75 million 60-kilogram bags of coffee were exported in September, which is down 18.4% from the same month last year. This makes it over 20% that shipments are off in the first three months of the marketing year versus the same time last year. The big decline comes as tariffs levied on Brazil by the U.S. are creating a barrier for Brazilian arabica coffee to enter. Coffee futures on the ICE are down 2%, to $3.7745 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0653 ET - Oil prices fall in afternoon trade, pressured by easing geopolitical risks in the Middle East and higher U.S. crude inventories. Brent slips 0.5% to $65.89 a barrel, while WTI is down 0.6% to $61.67 a barrel. Israel and Hamas agreed to the first phase of President Trump's peace plan for Gaza, a move that could reduce the region's risk premium. Meanwhile, U.S. crude inventories rose for a second straight week, sending bearish signals about demand in the world's largest oil consumer. Oil prices have fallen around 10% this year, as fears of an impending supply glut continue to weigh on sentiment. Citi analysts say sentiment remains "univocally bearish," with some doubting that a $60-a-barrel Brent floor will be enough to keep the market in balance. Others expect a more moderate price correction, with rising oil inventories outside key hubs expected to prevent sharp price drops. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0542 ET - The majority of the major policy shocks have now happened and the last quarter of 2025 will be dominated by how the world reacts, Aviva Investors says in a note. While the new tariff regime in the U.S. has been largely accepted by the rest of the world, the magnitude of the change should not be downplayed, it says. "With the major policy events likely now tailed off, the market must now digest how the aftershocks play out," it says. Within fixed income, Aviva thinks that front-end pricing is roughly fair for the central scenario and that term premia could widen further on rising global issuance and perceptions of risk to Federal Reserve independence. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0537 ET - U.S. Treasury yields are little changed in midday European trade in the absence of fresh impulses as the U.S. government shutdown continues. The two-year Treasury yield is up 0.7 basis points at 3.588%; the 10-year Tresaury yield is up 0.2 basis points at 4.132%, while the 30-year Treasury yield is down 0.2 basis points at 4.721%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0501 ET - European defense stocks fall in early trade after U.S. President Trump said that Israel and Hamas had agreed to the first phase of a cease-fire plan. This fall might be a small bout of profit-taking after a strong run for the defense sector, AJ Bell's investment director Russ Mould says. "Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 drove more investors to look at defense stocks and a subsequent heightening in geopolitical tensions in other parts of the world led various governments to spend more money on military and intelligence capabilities," he says. Babcock International falls 3.1%, Renk Group drops 2.8%, Saab is 2.15% lower, Hensoldt loses 1.5%, Leonardo and BAE Systems dip 1.2% each, while Rheinmetall and Thales are each down 0.9%. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: French OATs Lead Fall in Eurozone Government Bond Yields — Market Talk
data.description: Eurozone government bond yields decline, led by French OATs, with market focus on French President Emmanuel Macron's anticipated announcement of a new prime minister. "French politics will remain in the headlines with the president expected to convene with party representatives in the afternoon and likely to appoint a new prime minister thereafter--some think it could again be [Sebastien] Lecornu," ING senior rates strategists Michiel Tukker and Benjamin Schroeder say in a note. French OATs lead a fall in 10-year eurozone government bond yields, declining 3 basis points to 3.497%, according to Tradeweb. Meanwhile, 10-year German Bund yields decline 1.9 basis points to 2.686%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Euro's Tepid Recovery Looks Justified — Market Talk
data.description: The euro's tepid recovery following more positive headlines on French politics is understandable given current risks, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. French President Emmanuel Macron could name a new prime minister Friday to replace Sebastien Lecornu, fending off speculation about a snap election being called. However, there's a "general feeling that the political backing remains weak," Pesole says. "The market-appeasing pledge by outgoing PM Lecornu about delivering on budget obligations is hardly enough to price out French risk." The euro rises 0.1% to $1.1569 after reaching a two-month low of $1.1541 Thursday, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Jyske Bank at new all-time high after guidance hike
data.description:
** Shares in Jyske Bank jump around 5% to fresh record high after the Danish lender hiked its full-year guidance on Thursday

** Jyske Bank now expects net profit of 4.9 billion to 5.3 billion Danish crowns ($759 - $821 million), up from its previous forecast for 3.9 billion to 4.6 billion crowns

** Barclays says net interest income (NII) has reached its lowest point of the year in Q3, and points to Jyske Bank's strong surplus of capital, which could grow further if rules around commercial real estate are relaxed

** The broker sees these results as a positive read-across for Jyske's peer Danske Bank related to NII and trading

** The stock was up 4.3% at GMT 0724, topping the European STOXX 600 index and hitting a new intraday high of 760 crows

** Danske Bank is up 2.3%

($1 = 6.4551 Danish crowns)

data.title: China hits back at US ships with additional port fees
data.description:
Vessels owned or operated by U.S. firms and individuals - or those built in the United States or that fly the U.S. flag - will be charged additional port fees per voyage starting on October 14, China's transport ministry said.

The fees are a counter-measure against upcoming U.S. port fees, the ministry said on Friday.

Also starting on October 14, ships built in China - or operated or owned by Chinese entities - will need to pay a fee at their first port of call in the United States. Fees could top $1 million for a ship carrying over 10,000 containers, and could rise annually through 2028, according to analyst estimates.

Vessels owned or operated by a Chinese entity will face a flat fee of $80 per net tonnage per voyage to the U.S.

The U.S. fees on China-linked vessels, following a probe by the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR), are part of a broader U.S. effort to revive domestic shipbuilding and blunt China's naval and commercial shipping power.

Over the past two decades, China has catapulted itself to the No.1 position in the shipbuilding world, with its biggest shipyards handling both commercial and military projects.

The Chinese fees on U.S. vessels could hurt the U.S. less than the U.S. fees to be heaped on the legion of Chinese ships.

Last year, Chinese shipyards built more than 1,000 commercial vessels, while the U.S. constructed fewer than 10, according to military and industry analysts.

For U.S. vessels berthing at Chinese ports from October 14, the rate shall be 400 yuan ($56.13) per net tonne, the Chinese transport ministry said.

That will increase to 640 yuan ($89.81) from April 17, 2026, and to 880 yuan from April 17, 2027.

For vessels calling at Chinese ports from April 17, 2028, the charge shall be 1,120 yuan ($157.16) per net tonne.

Tensions between China and the United States have started to deepen since September, with the two superpowers looking to be struggling to move beyond their current trade tariff truce - a 90-day pause from August 11 that ends around November 9.

U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping are expected to meet as they attend an Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation ( APEC ) in South Korea at the end of October.

($1 = 7.1263 Chinese yuan renminbi)

About Greatland

Greatland is a gold and copper mining company listed on the Australian Securities Exchange and London Stock Exchange's AIM Market (ASX:GGP and AIM:GGP) and operates its business from Western Australia.

The Greatland portfolio includes the 100% owned Telfer mine, the adjacent 100% owned brownfield world-class Havieron gold-copper development project, and a significant exploration portfolio within the surrounding region. The combination of Telfer and Havieron provides for a substantial and long life gold-copper operation in the Paterson Province in the East Pilbara region of Western Australia.

data.title: Gold Holds Near $4,000 After Record High
data.description:
Spot gold hovered just below the $4,000 mark on Friday, as investors took a breather following a rally that lifted the precious metal to an all-time high of $4,059 on Wednesday.

The metal has risen about 52% this year and was on track for a 2.2% weekly gain—its eighth consecutive advance—driven by persistent geopolitical tensions, global economic uncertainty, and growing expectations of further US interest rate cuts.

While Israel’s government ratified a ceasefire with Hamas on Friday, paving the way to halt hostilities in Gaza within 24 hours, investors remain focused on broader headwinds, including slowing global growth, sticky inflation, and continued diversification away from US assets and the dollar.

Meanwhile, the US government shutdown is set to enter its third week, while political turmoil in France continues following the resignation of PM Lecornu.

On the monetary policy front, the Fed is widely expected to deliver 25 bps rate cuts in each of its remaining meetings this year.

data.title: Comex Gold Futures Has Likely Formed Interim Peak at $4,000/oz, Daily Chart Shows — Market Talk
data.description: Comex gold futures has likely formed an interim peak at $4,000/oz, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a research report. The commodity closed Thursday with a long bearish candlestick pattern on the daily chart, the analyst notes. Thursday's price action suggests an interim peak has emerged there, Chai says. With negative momentum picking up, the precious metal could pull back toward $3,900/oz, the analyst says. However, overall technical structure is still bullish and the 20-day simple moving average is trending upward to provide additional support. Following consolidation, the commodity will probably resume upside movements, Chai adds. Spot gold is 0.3% lower at $3,963.75/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Chinese Shares Fall Sharply Amid Profit Taking — Market Talk
data.description: Chinese shares ended sharply lower amid profit taking. The Shanghai Composite Index declined 0.9% to 3897.03, dropping below the key 3900 level. The Shenzhen Composite Index dropped 1.7% and the ChiNext Price Index shed 4.55% in its biggest daily percentage fall since April when the U.S. announced tariffs. That comes after tech and gold-mining stocks surged Thursday, catching up after China's eight-day Golden Week break as investors cheered news including spot gold hitting $4,000/oz and DeepSeek's new AI model. Investors have now likely started to book profit after a months-long rally. Cambricon ended 6.4% lower and Shandong Gold shed 4.65%. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: US Gasoline Futures Pull Back
data.description:
US gasoline futures fell to around $1.88 per gallon, nearing a 4½-year low of $1.85 reached on October 2, as easing geopolitical risks and ample supply pressured prices.

Markets reacted to news that Israel and Hamas agreed to a ceasefire and hostage-prisoner exchange deal, the first phase of a US-brokered peace plan to end the Gaza war.

The truce, which includes partial Israeli withdrawal from Gaza, reduced the geopolitical risk premium built into energy prices.

Meanwhile, OPEC+ agreed to raise oil production by 137,000 barrels per day in November, less than expected, temporarily calming supply fears but keeping the outlook bearish amid broader oversupply.

Additional crude flows from the US and other American producers have reinforced expectations of a year-end surplus.

Offsetting some optimism, Russia launched a massive drone and missile strike on Ukraine’s power infrastructure, causing blackouts in Kyiv and damaging gas facilities in Zaporizhzhia.

data.title: UK's Hays gains after first-quarter results meet expectations despite macro uncertainty
data.description:
** Shares of Hays up 2.15% at 58.89p

** Q1 group net fees down 8% y/y on a like-for-like basis, in line with company-compiled consensus of -8%

** RBC says it was 'something of a holding quarter' as macro outlook remains murky, especially in Europe, but some key areas of self-help delivery should provide reassurance to patient holders

** "Despite ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and challenging Perm conditions, we experienced a normal recovery in post-summer activity levels and trading was stable on a seasonally adjusted basis through quarter," says CEO

** YTD, stock down about 27.2%, including session's gains

data.title: Japan's Komeito Party Withdraws From Ruling Coalition
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--A major political ally of Japan's ruling Liberal Democratic Party has exited the governing coalition, ending a 26-year-long relationship in a move that could fuel political instability.

"There was a disagreement over the basic stance," regarding political funding issues that caused election losses, Komeito leader Tetsuo Saito said at a news conference Friday.

"Public trust in politics has been severely damaged," Saito said.

He said the party wouldn't support Sanae Takaichi, who was recently selected as the LDP's leader, as the nation's prime minister. Takaichi needs to gain approval in parliament to become the new premier.

The yen briefly strengthened to as high as 152.41 against the dollar before trading 0.2% higher at 152.78, FactSet data show.

  • Ronnie Harui contributed to this report

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

RNS Number : 9094C Water Intelligence PLC 10 October 2025  

Transaction in Own Shares and Total Voting Rights

Water Intelligence plc (AIM: WATR.L), a leading multinational provider of precision, minimally-invasive leak detection and remediation solutions for both potable and non-potable water, announces that on 9 October 2025, pursuant to the authority approved by shareholders at the Company's Annual General Meeting dated 7 October 2024, the Company purchased 15,000 ordinary shares of 1 penny each ("Ordinary Shares") at a price of 293.35 pence (the "Transaction").

Summary of Trades

In accordance with Article 5(1)(b) of the Market Abuse Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 (which forms part of domestic law pursuant to the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018), details of the purchase of its own ordinary shares by Water Intelligence, which were all executed through the Company's joint broker, Dowgate Capital, are set out below:

data.title: Ferrari Management Takes Conservative Stance With Guidance — Market Talk
data.description: Ferrari's new 2030 guidance came in below both Citi and consensus expectations, probably reflecting conservatism from management, Citi analysts write. The guidance looks for 2030 net revenue of around 9 billion euros, 10% below Visible Alpha consensus that was at 10 billion euros. Adjusted EBIT guidance of over 2.75 billion euros and a margin of over 30% is around 15% below consensus at 3.25 billion euros and a 32.3% margin. The targets are below Citi's "lower growth case" estimates compiled before yesterday's investor event. "We have argued that providing a growth guidance that was both achievable and sufficiently high would be difficult for management." Citi says there is some risk to both consensus EPS and multiples near-term. Shares rise 1% in Europe after falling 15% yesterday. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Embodied Intelligence Could Be Next Frontier for AI Commercialization — Market Talk
data.description: Embodied intelligence could be the next frontier for AI commercialization and competition, CGS International's Angela Cheng says in a research note. China has the advantage in the sector in terms of policy and supply chain, she says. Beijing could elevate embodied intelligence to a core strategic track supporting China's manufacturing powerhouse and digital nation initiatives in the next five-year plan, the analyst adds. CGS estimates that the market size of humanoid robots could reach 3 trillion yuan by 2040. As 2025 marks the first year of mass production of humanoid robots, the analyst suggests that investors focus on component makers with mass-production capabilities and key clients. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: India to reopen its embassy in Afghan capital Kabul
data.description:
India will reopen its embassy in the Afghan capital Kabul that was shut four years ago, Indian Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said on Friday, a significant move that expands the diplomatic links of the country governed by the Taliban.

India had closed its embassy in Kabul after the Taliban seized power following the withdrawal of U.S.-led NATO forces in 2021, but opened a small mission a year later to facilitate trade, medical support, and humanitarian aid.

About a dozen countries including China, Russia, Iran, Pakistan and Turkey have embassies operating in Kabul, although Russia is the only country to have formally recognised the Taliban administration.

New Delhi's announcement came as Afghan Taliban Foreign Minister Amir Khan Muttaqi began talks with Jaishankar on what is the first visit to India by a Taliban leader since 2021.

"India is fully committed to the sovereignty, territorial integrity and independence of Afghanistan," Jaishankar told Muttaqi in his opening remarks.

"Closer cooperation between us contributes to your national development, as well as regional stability and resilience," he said, adding that India's "technical mission" in Kabul was being upgraded to an embassy.

Jaishankar did not mention a timeline for the change.

Muttaqi is on a six-day visit to India aiming to boost ties with New Delhi. Analysts say the trip highlights Taliban efforts to expand engagement with regional powers in a quest for economic relations and eventual diplomatic recognition.

India and Afghanistan have historically had friendly ties, but New Delhi does not recognise the Taliban government.

Western diplomats have said the Taliban administration's path to recognition is being stalled by its curbs on women.

Talks between the two countries are expected to include discussions on political, economic and trade issues, the Afghan foreign ministry said this week.

Muttaqi's trip was made possible after the U.N. Security Council Committee temporarily lifted a travel ban on him to allow diplomatic engagements abroad.

He is among Afghan Taliban members who are under U.N. sanctions, including a travel ban and asset freeze. Temporary exemptions are sometimes granted for diplomacy.

data.title: European Stocks Waver
data.description:
The STOXX 50 rose 0.3%, while the STOXX 600 hovered around the flatline on Friday, capping off a muted week with limited market movement.

Traders remained cautious, awaiting new catalysts, assessing corporate updates, and preparing for the start of the earnings season.

The auto sector rebounded after having its worst day since March.

Stellantis gained nearly 2% after reporting a 13% increase in Q3 deliveries, driven largely by strong sales in North America.

Mercedes-Benz added 2.2%, and Volkswagen rose 0.9%.

For the week, the STOXX 50 is down 0.4%, while the STOXX 600 is up 0.1% so far.

data.title: India stocks, rupee, bonds swaps, call at 1:00 p.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was up 0.53% at 82,605, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 rose 0.54% to 25,318, led by financial stocks, while information technology stocks posted modest gains after Tata Consultancy Services beat quarterly revenue estimates.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee rose nearly 0.1% versus the U.S. dollar to 88.7, on the back of likely market interventions by the Reserve Bank of India, even as rising gold and silver prices added to the headwinds that the South Asian currency is facing.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN063335G=CC) was quoted at 98.62 rupees, with the yield flat at 6.5255% as traders awaited the weekly debt auction result.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was up flat at 5.4225%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) was up slightly at 5.6370%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.15%, and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.40%.
